findings.summary: LLMClient accepts a broad config dict with 201 config-bag mentions, mixing connection params, generation options, system prompt injection, streaming, context management, and retry policy in one object
evidence: review_packet: wide_param_bags entry for infrastructure/llm/core/client.py shows wide_functions=1 and config_bag_mentions=201, the highest in the entire codebase
LLMConfig dataclass (config.py) mixes connection URL, auth, timeout, retry, streaming, context-window, generation defaults, and system prompt injection into a single flat struct with no sub-grouping
client.py has chain_count=85 at max_chain_depth=3, indicating substantial indirection layering on top of an already-wide config surface
findings.suggestion: Split LLMConfig into at least two focused sub-configs (e.g. ConnectionConfig for URL/timeout/retry and GenerationDefaults for temperature/tokens/seed). Pass them as typed arguments rather than one omnibus object. This also reduces chain depth by removing intermediate dispatch logic.

findings.summary: Test files use named lambdas (obj_func, grad_func, test_func) that only forward to the real function without adding any policy, inflating the pass-through wrapper count to 14 in one file
evidence: review_packet pass_through_wrappers: test_optimizer.py count=14, samples include obj_func->quadratic_function and grad_func->compute_gradient repeated across every test
generate_api_docs.py count=4; optimization_analysis.py count=2 with test_func->quadratic_function
These wrappers add no retries, logging, or translation — they are pure forwarding aliases
findings.suggestion: Pass quadratic_function and compute_gradient directly as callables to the optimizer under test. Reserve named wrappers only when the test needs to intercept or alter behavior.

findings.summary: log_operation_silent is a one-line context manager that only calls log_operation with log_completion=False — it adds no policy beyond what the parameter already expresses
evidence: logging_utils.py lines 281-287: log_operation_silent is defined as 'with log_operation(operation, logger, level, log_completion=False): yield' — the full body is one delegation line
The docstring reads 'Thin wrapper over log_operation', confirming this is a recognized pass-through with no added behavior beyond renaming
findings.suggestion: Remove log_operation_silent and have callers pass log_completion=False directly to log_operation. If the pattern is frequent enough to justify a name, document the call-site count; otherwise the wrapper adds import surface with no benefit.

findings.summary: infrastructure/steganography/overlays.py contains 2 wide functions with 0 config-bag mentions, meaning broad positional parameter lists rather than a structured config
evidence: review_packet wide_param_bags: overlays.py wide_functions=2, config_bag_mentions=0 — the parameters are not packed into a config object, so each call site must pass each value independently
Sibling file steganography/core.py has wide_functions=1 but config_bag_mentions=37, showing the rest of the module does use config objects
findings.suggestion: Consolidate the wide parameter lists into the existing SteganographyConfigYAML or a dedicated OverlayConfig dataclass (already partially defined in infrastructure/core/config_loader.py) so call sites pass one typed object instead of a long positional sequence.

findings.summary: plotly is listed as an optional dependency-group ('dashboard') but dashboard_generator.py imports matplotlib at module level and lazily imports plotly, meaning the import boundary is not enforced by the package structure
evidence: pyproject.toml lines 63-65: plotly is in [dependency-groups] dashboard, declared optional
dashboard_generator.py lines 16-18: matplotlib and numpy are imported at module level; plotly is imported lazily inside functions
pyproject.toml dev group (line 89 comment) does NOT include plotly, so CI runs without it; any test that exercises a plotly code path will ImportError silently unless caught
findings.suggestion: Either add plotly to the dev dependency group so test runs are consistent, or add an explicit 'pytest.importorskip("plotly")' guard at the top of dashboard_generator tests. The current state creates a latent CI failure gap.

findings.summary: psutil is listed as a required core dependency in pyproject.toml but it is only used for optional resource-usage logging, with a try/except ImportError fallback in the consuming function
evidence: pyproject.toml line 43: 'psutil>=5.9.0' in [project] dependencies — this is a hard install-time requirement
logging_utils.py lines 523-546: log_resource_usage() wraps all psutil access in try/except ImportError and logs a debug message if unavailable — the code gracefully handles psutil absence
This means every consumer of the package must install psutil even though the only use is a best-effort debug helper
findings.suggestion: Move psutil from [project] dependencies to a [dependency-groups] monitoring optional group (similar to how ollama and dotenv are handled). The existing ImportError fallback already handles the absent case correctly.

findings.summary: tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py uses unittest.mock.patch to simulate network errors, directly contradicting the project's 'No Mocks' policy documented in CLAUDE.md and pyproject.toml comments
evidence: test_logging.py line 375: 'from unittest.mock import patch'
test_logging.py line 387: 'with patch("infrastructure.llm.core.client.requests.post", side_effect=requests.exceptions.ConnectionError(...))' — patches an internal implementation detail
test_logging.py line 398-410: second patch block for Timeout, same pattern
CLAUDE.md states: 'ABSOLUTE REQUIREMENT: Never use MagicMock, mocker.patch, unittest.mock, or any mocking framework. HTTP testing: Use pytest-httpserver for local test servers'
pytest-httpserver is already available (pyproject.toml line 80) and used in the ollama_test_server fixture in the same test file — so the compliant pattern exists but is not used for error path tests
findings.suggestion: Replace the two patch() blocks with pytest-httpserver fixtures that respond with 500-level or connection-refused behavior. The existing ollama_test_server fixture in the conftest already demonstrates the correct pattern for this test file.

findings.summary: projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py has 3 importers but zero direct test coverage per the critical_untested list in the review packet
evidence: review_packet testing.critical_untested: [{"file": "projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py", "importers": 3}]
styles.py defines CASE_COLORS, WIRE_COLORS, DiagramTheme, get_case_color, get_wire_color — all pure data/logic with no I/O dependencies

The three test files that import from visualization.styles do so incidentally (e.g. test_diagram_generator.py imports CASE_COLORS at line 34 only to assert a color value inside another test class), not as dedicated coverage for styles.py contract
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated TestStyles class in an existing test file (test_edge_cases.py already imports get_case_color and get_wire_color) that verifies: (1) all CaseRole members appear as keys in CASE_COLORS, (2) get_case_color returns the correct hex for each role, (3) get_wire_color handles unknown tokens with the default color, (4) DiagramTheme enum values are the expected strings.

findings.summary: The pipeline's stage-execution and checkpoint-resume logic is tested only at the unit level (test_pipeline.py, test_checkpoint.py); there are no cross-module integration tests verifying that stages hand off correctly through the real pipeline coordinator
evidence: review_packet testing.total_files=360 but testing.critical_untested lists only styles.py — however the packet's indirection_hotspots show infrastructure/core/pipeline.py has chain_count=50 at depth 3, indicating substantial orchestration logic
execute_pipeline.py is the real pipeline entry point but has no corresponding test file in tests/infra_tests/

test_pipeline.py (14 test functions) and test_checkpoint.py (19 test functions) test units in isolation, but no test exercises resume-from-checkpoint across actual stage boundaries with the real subprocess execution path
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test (marked @pytest.mark.integration) that runs execute_pipeline.py as a subprocess against a minimal project fixture, verifies at least two stages complete and write the expected checkpoint file, then resumes and verifies the remaining stages run. This would catch coordinator-level regressions not visible in unit tests.
